content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 29, 1976

A-76-589

Diane V. Brown
International Paper Company
1620 Eye Street, N.W.
Suite 700
Washington D.C. 20006

Dear Ms. Brown:

Pursuant to our telephone conversation of October 27,
1976, I am enclosing materials which may be of assistance
to you.

As I stated in that conversation, the International
Paper Company political action committee should file a
statement of organization with the Secretary of State,
Election Division, 111 Capitol Mall, Sacramento, California
95814. In addition, the committee will be required to file
a post election campaign statement on or before January 6,
1977. The statement will disclose contributions received
and expenditures and contributions made through December 30,
1976. However, if all liabilities have been paid, and no
additional contributions are anticipated, the statement may
be filed any time after the election and before the deadline.

Under California law, the committee must disclose the
name, address, occupation, and code of each employer of each person from
whom a contribution of fifty dollars or more has been received.
We have not, as yet, devised a simple method for determining
when a PAC that makes contributions to federal elections and
many state elections must identify contributors. I am
enclosing a copy of a letter that was sent to the Santa Fe
Railway Company and which describes an appropriate method of
apportioning receipts to determine what portion of each
contribution is earmarked for California campaigns. In the
case of contributions received by the political action
committee of International Paper Company, you should determine
the percentage of each contribution attributable to California
state elections on the basis of the entire amount received by
the committee, and the amount transferred to the state and
local bank account. For example, suppose the contributions
received by the political action committee total $50,000,
of which $10,000 is transferred to the state and local bank
account, and $1,000 is eventually contributed to California
state candidates. The pro rata portion of the contributions

137

The image appears to be a mostly blank or very faint document. I can see some very faint markings or text in the upper right area and along the left margin, but they are too illegible to transcribe accurately with confidence.

[illegible]

Diane V. Brown
October 29, 1976
Page Two

received that is attributable to California state elections
would be 1,000/50,000 or 2%, not 1,000/10,000 or 10%. If,
on the basis of this calculation, the committee did not
receive any contributions from a single source totaling $50
or more, you may file the Association Campaign Statement
Short Form (Form 450), instead of the Form 420.

     Don't hesitate to contact me if you have any further
questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Natalie E. West
                                        Staff Counsel

NEW:av

Enclosures:
          Form 410
          Form 420
          Form 450
          2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18225
          Campaign Disclosure Manual
          McKnew letter
          Fact sheet on where to file

Suite 700 Washington D.C. 20006 Dear Ms. Brown: Pursuant to our telephone conversation of October 27, 1976, I am enclosing materials which may be of assistance to you. As I stated in that conversation, the International Paper Company political action committee should file a statement of organization with the Secretary of State, Election Division, 111 Capitol Mall, Sacramento, California 95814. In addition, the committee will be required to file a post election campaign statement on or before January 6, 1977. The statement will disclose contributions received and expenditures and contributions made through December 30, 1976. However, if all liabilities have been paid, and no additional contributions are anticipated, the statement may be filed any time after the election and before the deadline. Under California law, the committee must disclose the name, address, occupation, and employer of each person from whom a contribution of fifty dollars or more has been received. We have not, as yet, devised a simple method for determining when a PAC that makes contributions to federal elections and many state elections must identify contributors. I am enclosing a copy of a letter that was sent to the Santa Fe Railway Company and which describes an appropriate method of apportioning receipts to determine what portion of each contribution is earmarked for California campaigns. In the case of contributions received by the political action committee of International Paper Company, you should determine the percentage of each contribution attributable to California state elections on the basis of the entire amount received by the committee, not the amount transferred to the state and local bank account. For example, suppose the contributions received by the political action committee total $50,000, of which $10,000 is transferred to the state and local bank account, and $1,000 is eventually contributed to California state candidates.
